questions.stem: A map $T : X \to Y$ between vector spaces is a linear operator when it satisfies which property for all vectors and scalars?
options: $T(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha T x + \beta T y$.
questions.explanation: Linearity means $T$ preserves linear combinations: $T(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha Tx + \beta Ty$. Equivalently $T$ is additive and homogeneous. Preserving products or norms is not what linearity requires.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A linear operator $T : X \to Y$ between normed spaces is called bounded when which condition holds?
options: There is a constant $c$ with $\|T x\| \le c\,\|x\|$ for all $x$.
$T x$ is a bounded sequence for each $x$.
questions.explanation: Boundedness of a linear operator means there exists $c \ge 0$ with $\|Tx\| \le c\,\|x\|$ for all $x$. The smallest such $c$ is the operator norm $\|T\|$. This is unrelated to being onto or one-to-one.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: For a linear operator between normed spaces, boundedness is equivalent to which analytic property?
questions.explanation: A linear operator is bounded if and only if it is continuous. In fact for linear maps continuity at a single point (for instance at $0$) already forces continuity everywhere, and that is equivalent to the bound $\|Tx\| \le c\|x\|$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The operator norm of a bounded linear operator $T$ is defined as which quantity?
questions.explanation: The operator norm is $\|T\| = \sup_{x \ne 0} \|Tx\| / \|x\| = \sup_{\|x\| = 1} \|Tx\|$. It is the least constant $c$ in the bound $\|Tx\| \le c\|x\|$. Using an infimum or evaluating at $0$ would give the wrong notion.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The fundamental inequality satisfied by the operator norm is which of the following?
questions.explanation: By the definition of the operator norm as a supremum, $\|Tx\| \le \|T\|\,\|x\|$ holds for every $x$. This submultiplicative-style bound is the workhorse inequality for bounded operators.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: On a finite-dimensional normed space, which linear operators are bounded?
options: Only the identity and its scalar multiples.
questions.explanation: Every linear operator from a finite-dimensional normed space is bounded (hence continuous). This rests on the equivalence of all norms in finite dimensions. Unboundedness of linear operators is a purely infinite-dimensional phenomenon.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the operator norm of the diagonal operator $T(x_1, x_2) = (2x_1, -3x_2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ with the Euclidean norm.
questions.explanation: For a diagonal operator the Euclidean operator norm is the largest absolute diagonal entry: $\|T\| = \max(|2|, |-3|) = 3$. The bound $\|Tx\|^2 = 4x_1^2 + 9x_2^2 \le 9\|x\|^2$ is attained at $x = (0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the operator norm of the scalar multiplication operator $T x = 5x$ on any normed space.
questions.explanation: Since $\|Tx\| = \|5x\| = 5\|x\|$, the ratio $\|Tx\|/\|x\| = 5$ for every nonzero $x$, so $\|T\| = 5$. Scalar multiplication by $\alpha$ has operator norm $|\alpha|$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: What is the operator norm of the identity operator $I$ on a nonzero normed space?
options: Equal to the dimension of the space.
questions.explanation: Since $\|Ix\| = \|x\|$, the ratio $\|Ix\|/\|x\| = 1$ for all nonzero $x$, so $\|I\| = 1$. The identity is the prototypical isometry.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the operator norm of the Volterra integration operator $(Tf)(x) = \int_0^x f(t)\, dt$ on $C[0,1]$ with the supremum norm.
questions.explanation: For $\|f\|_\infty \le 1$ we have $|(Tf)(x)| \le \int_0^x |f| \le x \le 1$, so $\|Tf\|_\infty \le 1$ and $\|T\| \le 1$. Taking $f \equiv 1$ gives $(Tf)(x) = x$ with $\|Tf\|_\infty = 1$, so $\|T\| = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The set $B(X, Y)$ of all bounded linear operators from $X$ to $Y$, with the operator norm, is a vector space that is complete (a Banach space) under which condition on $Y$?
questions.explanation: $B(X,Y)$ with the operator norm is itself a normed space, and it is complete precisely when the target $Y$ is complete. Completeness of the domain $X$ is not needed. In particular the dual space $X^* = B(X, \mathbb{K})$ is always a Banach space since the scalar field is complete.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The operator norm is submultiplicative for composition, meaning which inequality holds for $S, T$?
questions.explanation: For composable bounded operators, $\|ST\| \le \|S\|\,\|T\|$, since $\|STx\| \le \|S\|\,\|Tx\| \le \|S\|\,\|T\|\,\|x\|$. Equality need not hold in general.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Why is the differentiation operator $D f = f'$ from $(C^1[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ into $(C[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ unbounded?
options: It maps continuous functions to discontinuous ones.
The functions $f_n(t) = \sin(n t)$ have $\|f_n\|_\infty = 1$ but $\|D f_n\|_\infty = n \to \infty$.
questions.explanation: Differentiation is linear but unbounded with respect to the sup norm: $f_n(t) = \sin(nt)$ satisfies $\|f_n\|_\infty = 1$, while $f_n'(t) = n\cos(nt)$ has $\|f_n'\|_\infty = n$, which grows without bound. No constant $c$ can satisfy $\|Df\| \le c\|f\|$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: On $\ell^2$, the diagonal (multiplication) operator $T(x_n) = (a_n x_n)$ defined by a bounded scalar sequence $(a_n)$ has operator norm equal to which quantity?
questions.explanation: The diagonal operator on $\ell^2$ given by $(a_n)$ has $\|T\| = \sup_n |a_n|$. The upper bound follows from $\|Tx\|^2 = \sum |a_n|^2 |x_n|^2 \le (\sup_n |a_n|)^2 \|x\|^2$, and testing on standard basis vectors $e_k$ shows it is attained in the limit.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The kernel (null space) of a bounded linear operator $T : X \to Y$ has which property?
options: It is a closed subspace of $X$.
questions.explanation: The kernel $\ker T = \{ x : Tx = 0 \} = T^{-1}(\{0\})$ is the preimage of the closed set $\{0\}$ under a continuous map, hence closed; it is also a subspace by linearity. It need not be trivial.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the operator norm of $T(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (3x_1, 0, -1 \cdot x_3)$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$ with the Euclidean norm.
questions.explanation: This is a diagonal operator with entries $3, 0, -1$. Its Euclidean operator norm is the largest absolute entry, $\max(3, 0, 1) = 3$, attained on the first coordinate direction.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A bounded linear operator is also called a bounded linear transformation; which of the following is an equivalent way to compute $\|T\|$?
options: $\|T\| = \|T e_1\|$ for a fixed basis vector.
questions.explanation: Equivalent formulas for the operator norm are $\|T\| = \sup_{\|x\| \le 1} \|Tx\| = \sup_{\|x\| = 1} \|Tx\| = \sup_{x \ne 0} \|Tx\|/\|x\|$. Using an infimum or a single basis vector generally gives the wrong value.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: If $T : X \to Y$ is a bounded linear operator and $x_n \to x$ in $X$, what can be said about $T x_n$?
options: $T x_n \to T x$, because $T$ is continuous.
$T x_n$ converges only if $T$ is injective.
questions.explanation: Bounded equals continuous for linear operators, so convergence is preserved: $\|Tx_n - Tx\| = \|T(x_n - x)\| \le \|T\|\,\|x_n - x\| \to 0$. Hence $Tx_n \to Tx$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: On $\ell^2$, the right shift operator $S(x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots) = (0, x_1, x_2, \dots)$ has operator norm equal to which value?
questions.explanation: The right shift inserts a leading zero and preserves all other entries, so $\|Sx\|_2 = \|x\|_2$ for every $x$. It is an isometry into $\ell^2$, hence $\|S\| = 1$ (it is not surjective, but the norm is still $1$).
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Which of the following operators is NOT bounded?
options: A scalar multiple $\alpha I$ on a normed space.
questions.explanation: Differentiation under the sup norm is unbounded, as the functions $\sin(nt)$ show ($\|f_n\|_\infty = 1$, $\|f_n'\|_\infty = n$). The identity, scalar multiples, and the Volterra integration operator (norm $1$) are all bounded.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: For a bounded linear operator $T$, having operator norm $\|T\| = 0$ means which of the following?
options: $T$ is the zero operator ($T x = 0$ for all $x$).
questions.explanation: If $\|T\| = 0$ then $\|Tx\| \le 0 \cdot \|x\| = 0$ for all $x$, so $Tx = 0$ for every $x$; that is $T$ is the zero operator. The operator norm is a genuine norm on $B(X,Y)$, vanishing only at the zero operator.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A linear operator $T$ between normed spaces is continuous at every point as soon as it is continuous where?
options: At every point of the unit sphere.
Nowhere; continuity must be checked at all points separately.
questions.explanation: For a linear operator, continuity at the origin implies continuity everywhere: $\|Tx - Tx_0\| = \|T(x - x_0)\|$, so control near $0$ transfers to every point. This is why one only needs the single bound $\|Tx\| \le c\|x\|$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The range (image) of a bounded linear operator $T : X \to Y$ is always which of the following?
options: A subspace of $Y$, but not necessarily closed.
questions.explanation: By linearity the range is a subspace of $Y$, but it need not be closed: for instance a diagonal operator on $\ell^2$ with entries $1/n$ has a dense, non-closed range. (Closedness of the range is the content of stronger results such as the closed range theorem.)
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Let $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ be the projection $T(x_1, x_2) = (x_1, 0)$, using the Euclidean norm. What is $\|T\|$?
questions.explanation: For this projection $\|Tx\|^2 = x_1^2 \le x_1^2 + x_2^2 = \|x\|^2$, so $\|T\| \le 1$; equality holds for $x = (1,0)$. Hence $\|T\| = 1$. Any nonzero orthogonal projection on a Hilbert space has norm $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Find the Euclidean operator norm of the diagonal operator $T(x_1, x_2) = (2x_1, -3x_2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, and of the scalar operator $S x = 5x$.
questions.solution: For a diagonal operator the Euclidean norm is the largest absolute diagonal entry, so $\|T\| = \max(2, 3)$, which equals {{1}}. For the scalar operator $S x = 5x$, the norm is $\|S\|$, which equals {{2}}. Since $\|T\| < \|S\|$, the operator $T$ has the {{3}} norm of the two.
questions.explanation: The diagonal operator norm is $\max(|2|, |-3|) = 3$; the scalar operator $5x$ has norm $5$. Since $3 < 5$, $T$ has the smaller norm.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Determine the operator norm of the Volterra integration operator $(Tf)(x) = \int_0^x f(t)\, dt$ on $C[0,1]$ with the supremum norm.
questions.solution: For $\|f\|_\infty \le 1$ and $x \in [0,1]$ we have $|(Tf)(x)| \le \int_0^x 1\, dt = x \le 1$, so $\|T\| \le 1$. Testing the constant function $f \equiv 1$ gives $(Tf)(x) = x$, whose sup norm on $[0,1]$ is {{1}}. Hence the operator norm $\|T\|$ equals {{2}}, and this operator {{3}} bounded.
questions.explanation: The bound gives $\|T\| \le 1$, and $f \equiv 1$ produces $Tf(x) = x$ with sup norm $1$, so $\|T\| = 1$. A finite norm means $T$ is bounded.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Recall the basic facts: the bounded-continuous equivalence, the operator-norm inequality, and the identity operator.
questions.solution: For a linear operator, being bounded is equivalent to being {{1}}. Every bounded operator satisfies $\|T x\| \le \|T\|\,\|x\|$, so the norm controls the operator from {{2}}. The identity operator on a nonzero normed space has operator norm equal to {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Bounded equals continuous for linear operators. The inequality $\|Tx\| \le \|T\|\,\|x\|$ bounds $\|Tx\|$ from above. The identity has operator norm $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Reason about the space $B(X, Y)$ and the differentiation operator under the sup norm.
questions.solution: The space $B(X, Y)$ of bounded linear operators with the operator norm is complete (a Banach space) when the target space $Y$ {{1}} complete. The differentiation operator on $(C^1[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ into $(C[0,1], \|\cdot\|_\infty)$ {{2}} bounded, since $\sin(nt)$ has sup norm $1$ but derivative sup norm $n$. The number of the operators (identity, a scalar multiple, Volterra integration) that are bounded is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $B(X,Y)$ is complete when $Y$ is complete. Differentiation under the sup norm is not bounded. The identity, a scalar multiple, and Volterra integration are all bounded, so the count is $3$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis
